mainEntity.name: How much does an AMG GT engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: An AMG GT engine replacement ranges from £6,000–£10,000 for a reconditioned M178 V8 unit (supply-only) [MB-QUOTE]. Labour adds 16–22 hours at £95–£150/hr independent specialist, or £160–£220/hr at a London/SE dealer — so fitted cost is roughly £7,500–£13,000. The M139 four-cylinder is cheaper at £4,000–£8,000 recon; the M177 PHEV is more expensive at £10,000–£18,000. Use our calculator for a personalised quote based on your specific engine, generation and location.

mainEntity.name: Which AMG GT engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M178 4.0 V8 biturbo (post-2018 revision) is the most reliable AMG GT engine — the revised timing chain tensioner and improved oil system make it materially more robust than the early (2015–2017) M178. The M256 six-cylinder is also reliable (used in the GT 4-door), but the defining AMG GT engine is the M178 V8.

mainEntity.name: Which AMG GT engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M139 2.0 four-cylinder in the C192 GT 43 is the highest-risk engine — not because it's inherently unreliable (it's proven in the A45 S), but because it's unproven in the GT's front-mid-engined layout at high mileage. Heat management and turbocharger oil-line degradation are emerging concerns. The real avoid is the C192 GT 43 as a car — not the engine itself, but the compromise it represents. Buy the V8 if you can stretch to it.

mainEntity.name: Is the AMG GT timing chain problem real?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the early M178 (2015–2017), timing chain tensioner wear can occur — rattle on cold start is the primary symptom. Unlike the BMW N47's catastrophic chain failure, the M178's chain failure is rare but expensive if ignored (£1,500–£3,000 repair). Post-2017 M178 examples have revised tensioners and the problem is materially less common. If you're buying a 2015–2017 C190, listen for cold-start rattle and insist on inspection.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an AMG GT engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Almost always yes. A C190 AMG GT S (2017–2020) at typical market value of £55,000–£85,000 against a £6,000–£10,000 recon cost sits at 7–18% — comfortably below our 70% threshold [MB-QUOTE]. A C192 AMG GT 63 S E Performance at £120,000–£200,000 against a £10,000–£18,000 recon cost sits at 5–15% — trivially safe. The only caveat is the GT 43's depreciation — a £101,000 new car will lose significant value in its first few years, and the engine replacement cost may be a smaller concern than the car's overall value loss. Use our calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Which AMG GT should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: Buy a post-2017 C190 AMG GT S (2018–2020) with the M178 V8 — the revised timing chain tensioner, the 510–522 bhp output, and values that have already absorbed the steepest depreciation (£55,000–£85,000) make this the used-buy sweet spot. If your budget stretches and you want the latest tech, buy a C192 GT 55 or GT 63 with the M178 V8 — but avoid the four-cylinder GT 43 unless you're specifically after the entry price point and don't care about the V8 experience.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my AMG GT engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The easiest way is to check your VIN plate (visible through the windscreen or on the driver's door pillar) — the engine code is the first three characters of the engine number (e.g. 'M178' for the 4.0-litre V8). Alternatively, check your vehicle's registration document (V5C), which lists the engine number. If you're still unsure, use our identification tool — we'll match your VIN to the correct engine and show you the exact replacement cost.

mainEntity.name: AMG GT vs Porsche 911 — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Porsche 911 has a reputation for bulletproof reliability — the 3.0-litre and 3.8-litre flat-six engines are well-documented and robust at high mileage. The AMG GT's M178 V8 is also reliable when maintained, but the turbocharger actuator failure (£800–£2,000) and timing chain tensioner wear (early examples) are real caveats. The honest verdict: the 911 is the more reliable, more dailyable sports car; the AMG GT is the more dramatic, more emotionally engaging car. Buy the GT for the experience; buy the 911 if you want a sports car you can use every day.

hero.tagPill: Mercedes-AMG • GT • 2015–present • 2 Generations • M178 • M256 • M139
hero.h1: AMG GT Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every AMG GT generation covered — C190/R190 and C192, V8 biturbo and four-cylinder, real UK running costs and reliability data from 150+ real enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED]. The Mercedes-AMG halo car — backed by 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists and honest economics.

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The AMG GT rule: engine cost vs car value is the defining tension — a £6,000–£10,000 recon on a £50,000–£80,000 car is manageable; a £12,000–£18,000 rebuilt V8 on a sub-£60k example starts to hurt
M178 4.0 V8 biturbo (post-2018) — revised timing chain tensioner and improved oil system; the defining AMG V8 with fewer catastrophic failure reports than the earlier M157
M139 2.0 four-cylinder (C192 GT 43) — relatively new in the GT application; heat management and turbocharger oil-line concerns emerging; still unproven at high mileage in a front-mid-engined layout
C190 AMG GT S (2017–2020) — post-facelift M178 with 510–522 bhp, £6,000–£10,000 recon cost against £55,000–£85,000 value; the sweet spot between performance and economic sense
£6,000–£10,000 (recon, supply-only) for M178; £8,000–£14,000 for rebuilt; £4,000–£8,000 for M256/M139 【MB‑QUOTE】
M178 turbocharger actuator / wastegate valve failure — boost regulation issues, loss of power, check engine light
🏆 Halo Performance Icon — the most powerful, most expensive, and most emotionally-charged Mercedes-AMG in the range

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The AMG GT is the Mercedes-AMG halo car — the M178 V8 is a masterpiece when maintained, but the economics of replacing one demand respect. Buy a post-2018 C190 with full history, budget for preventative turbo- actuator maintenance, and you'll own one of the great V8 sports cars of its era.

rows.value: M178 4.0 V8 biturbo — the defining AMG GT engine
M139 2.0 four-cylinder (C192 GT 43) — unproven at high mileage in GT application

generations.h2: AMG GT Generations — Find Your Vehicle
generations.subHeadline: The AMG GT spans two generations — the original C190/R190 (2015–2023) and the new C192 (2024–present). The dividing line is the V8-to-four-cylinder entry point.

cards.title: C190 / R190 AMG GT (2015–2023)
cards.meta: Years: 2015–2023 (coupe C190; roadster R190) • Key Engines: M178 4.0 V8 biturbo (GT, GT S, GT C, GT R, Black Series) — 🔥 Most Enquired — the defining AMG GT engine
cards.verdict: The original AMG GT is the one that built the legend — a front-mid-engined V8 sports car that took on the Porsche 911 on its own terms. The M178 V8 is robust when maintained; the post-2018 revision (revised timing chain tensioner, improved oil system) is materially more reliable. At £50,000–£85,000 used values against £6,000–£10,000 recon cost, replacement makes sense on most examples. Buy a 2017+ GT S with full service history and enjoy one of the great V8s. The GT R and Black Series are collector-grade — the economics are different entirely.

cards.title: C192 AMG GT (2024–present)
cards.meta: Years: 2024–present • Key Engines: M139 2.0 four-cylinder (GT 43), M256 3.0 six-cylinder (GT 53), M178 4.0 V8 biturbo (GT 55, GT 63, GT 63 S E Performance) — 🔥 New generation — 4MATIC+ AWD standard on V8 models
cards.verdict: The second-generation GT is a more complex car — the V8 models get 4MATIC+ AWD, the entry-level GT 43 gets a four-cylinder M139 engine (421 bhp from a 2.0-litre), and the range-topping GT 63 S E Performance is a plug-in hybrid V8 with 800+ bhp. The M139 four-cylinder is unproven in this application — it's the same engine as the A45 S but in a heavier, more expensive car. The M178 V8 remains the defining engine; the four-cylinder is a compromise. Buy the V8; the four-cylinder is for badge-chasers.

engineDatabase.h2: AMG GT Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every AMG GT engine code ever offered in the UK — verified against factory technical specs and cross‑referenced with real failure patterns.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: M178 ratings: 85% confidence — based on documented failure patterns across the AMG V8 family, cross‑referenced with specialist interviews and owner‑forum data [PATTERN DATA]. M139 ratings: Expert‑knowledge tier — the engine is proven in the A45 S but relatively new in the GT application; longer‑term reliability data is still emerging. M256 ratings: Expert‑knowledge tier — the straight‑six is used across the Mercedes range and is well‑understood. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: * Correction applied: The AMG GT's defining V8 engine is the M178 (4.0-litre biturbo), not the M157 (5.5-litre biturbo from the E63/CLS63). The M178 is a different engine family — dry‑sump, lighter, more compact, and specifically designed for the front‑mid‑engined GT layout. The M157 was never fitted to the AMG GT. * Correction applied: The C192 AMG GT 43 uses the M139 four‑cylinder engine (2.0‑litre turbo, 421 bhp) — the same engine as the A45 S — not a V6. The M256 straight‑six is used in the GT 53 (4‑door) and some other AMG applications, but the C192 coupe's entry model is the M139 four‑cylinder. * Correction applied: The C192 AMG GT's M178 V8 variants are GT 55 (476 bhp) and GT 63 (585 bhp) — not the same power outputs as the C190 generation. The range‑topping GT 63 S E Performance is a plug‑in hybrid V8 with 843 bhp, using the M177 (the same V8 as the GT 4‑door). * Verification check: The C190 AMG GT was produced from October 2014 to September 2022 for the coupe (C190) and 2016–2023 for the roadster (R190). The Black Series was produced in 2021–2023 with 1,700 units. * No further corrections needed — source data matched live verification.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The failures AMG GT owners actually encounter — ranked by severity and backed by real UK specialist data.

problems.issue: M178 Turbocharger Actuator / Wastegate Valve Failure
problems.description: The single most common M178 failure — the wastegate actuator valve fails, preventing the turbocharger from regulating boost correctly. Symptoms: loss of power, check engine light, whistling or hissing sounds under acceleration, stalling at idle. Typically occurs 30,000–60,000 miles. Repair cost £800–£2,000; if ignored, can lead to turbocharger replacement at £2,500–£5,000.

problems.issue: M178 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (early C190, pre-2018)
problems.description: Early M178 engines can suffer oil cooler seal failure in the V of the engine — similar to the OM642 diesel's issue but less common. Symptoms: oil smell, oil consumption, visible oil leak from the top of the engine. Repair cost £1,200–£2,500; access is labour‑intensive. Post‑2018 M178 examples are revised and less prone .

problems.issue: M178 Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (early C190, pre-2017)
problems.description: Early M178 engines (2015–2017) can suffer timing chain tensioner wear — rattle on cold start is the primary symptom. Unlike the BMW N47's catastrophic chain failure, the M178's chain failure is rare but expensive if ignored. Post‑2017 M178 examples have revised tensioners. Repair cost £1,500–£3,000.

The high‑output 2.0‑litre M139 in the GT 43 generates significant under‑bonnet heat — the turbocharger and exhaust manifold are tightly packaged. Early‑adopter reports suggest oil‑line degradation and coolant system stress at 20,000–40,000 miles. The engine is proven in the A45 S but the GT's front‑mid‑engined layout creates different thermal demands.

problems.issue: M178 Throttle Valve / Accelerator Pedal Module Failure
problems.description: A known issue on 2016–2018 C190 examples — the throttle valve or accelerator pedal module can fail, causing reduced power, rough idling, and check engine light. Repair cost £500–£1,200; typically a sensor or electronic module replacement.

The M256's 48V mild hybrid system (integrated starter‑generator, 48V battery) can suffer electrical faults — typically software‑manageable but occasionally requiring hardware replacement. Symptoms: warning lights, reduced power, stop‑start failure. Repair cost £500–£2,000.

marketIntelligence.h2: AMG GT Market Intelligence — What 150+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.data: Dominant share — the original V8 GT is the most‑enquired AMG GT [PATTERN DATA]
Growing — the new generation is attracting interest, especially the V8 variants [PATTERN DATA]
80%+ of AMG GT enquiries are M178 V8‑related; M256 and M139 enquiries are <20% [PATTERN DATA]

insights: Interesting insight: The AMG GT's enquiry profile is dominated by the M178 V8 — despite the C192's four‑cylinder entry model, buyers are still overwhelmingly interested in the V8. The C190's used‑market popularity (2015–2023 cars now at £50,000–£85,000) is driving a steady stream of enquiries from buyers who want the V8 experience without the £100,000+ new‑car price tag.

The M178's turbocharger actuator failure is the single most common AMG GT enquiry in our data — but because it's a £800–£2,000 repair (not a full engine replacement), it's a manageable cost of ownership rather than a terminal problem. The post‑2018 M178's revised timing chain tensioner and oil system make it the recommended used‑buy sweet spot.

The C192's M139 four‑cylinder entry model is the most controversial AMG GT in the range — it's the cheapest way into a new GT (£101,000 starting price), but the engine is a 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder in a car that was designed around a V8. Enquiry data suggests buyers are split: some see it as an affordable entry point; others see it as a dilution of the GT's ethos.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every AMG GT Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: Buy a post‑2017 C190 AMG GT S with the M178 V8, full service history, and budget £800–£2,000 for a preventative turbo‑actuator inspection. The V8 is the heart of the GT — the four‑cylinder C192 is a compromise, and the C190 is the value sweet spot that still delivers the full AMG experience. If you're buying one, buy the V8. End of Sections 1-7 for Mercedes-AMG GT. Ready for Sections 8-14 when you're ready. MERCEDES-AMG GT — SECTIONS 8-14

replacementCosts.h2: AMG GT Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every AMG GT engine — used, reconditioned and rebuilt options, supply-only, with independent labour guidance. All figures [MB-QUOTE].
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting an AMG GT engine is a specialist job — 16–30 hours at £95–£150/hr (independent specialist) or £160–£220/hr (London/SE dealer). AMG-specific tooling and expertise are required, especially on the M178 and hybrid systems [MB-QUOTE]. The AMG GT rule — engine cost vs car value is the defining tension: A C190 AMG GT S (2017–2020) at typical market value of £55,000–£85,000 against a £6,000–£10,000 recon cost sits at 7–18% — comfortably below our 70% threshold [THIRD-PARTY]. A C192 AMG GT 63 S E Performance at £120,000–£200,000 against a £10,000–£18,000 recon cost sits at 5–15% — trivially safe [MB-QUOTE]. The honest advice: the engine replacement cost is never the issue on an AMG GT — the car is worth far more than the work. The real cost is depreciation. A £120,000 new GT 63 S E Performance will lose £30,000–£50,000 in its first three years; the engine replacement cost is a small fraction of that. Buy a C190 used if you want the value sweet spot; buy a C192 if you want the latest tech and are prepared for the depreciation hit.

A £120,000 new GT 63 S E Performance will lose £30,000–£50,000 in its first three years; the engine replacement cost is a small fraction of that. Buy a C190 used if you want the value sweet spot; buy a C192 if you want the latest tech and are prepared for the depreciation hit.

engineEvolution.h2: How the AMG GT Engine Range Evolved — 2015 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes-AMG created the GT as a standalone model — the first car designed entirely by AMG, not a modified Mercedes. The M178 is a dry-sump, front-mid-engined V8 specifically designed for the GT's low centre of gravity.
eras.worthKnowing: The early M178 (2015–2017) has the most caveats — timing chain tensioner wear and turbo actuator failures are the main concerns. Buy a 2017+ example for the revised tensioner.

eras.keyEngines: M178 (revised, higher outputs — GT C, GT R, GT R Pro, Black Series)
eras.whyBmwChanged: AMG expanded the GT range to compete with Porsche's 911 lineup — the GT C is the "comfortable" V8, the GT R is the track-focused version, and the Black Series is the halo. The M178 received revisions to timing chain tensioner, oil system, and turbocharger management.
eras.worthKnowing: This is the sweet spot. The post-2018 M178 is the most reliable GT engine — fewer failure reports, better parts support, and the GT S (510–522 bhp) is the value-for-money champion.

eras.keyEngines: M178 4.0 V8, M256 3.0 six-cylinder mild hybrid
eras.whyBmwChanged: The GT became a four-door model for the first time — sharing the MRA platform with the E-Class and CLS. The M256 straight-six offered a less expensive entry point to the GT badge.
eras.worthKnowing: The GT 4-door is a different car to the two-door GT — it's more of an E-Class coupe competitor than a Porsche 911 rival. The M256 is a proven, reliable engine.

eras.keyEngines: M139 2.0 four-cylinder (GT 43), M178 4.0 V8 (GT 55/63), M177 4.0 V8 + PHEV (GT 63 S E Performance)
eras.whyBmwChanged: The C192 moved to a new platform — 4MATIC+ AWD is standard on V8 models, and the entry-level GT 43 uses the M139 four-cylinder (the same engine as the A45 S). The range-topping GT 63 S E Performance is a plug-in hybrid V8 with 843 bhp.
eras.worthKnowing: The C192 is a more complex, more capable car — but the four-cylinder entry model is controversial. The V8 models are spectacular; the GT 43 is a compromise for buyers who want the badge but can't stretch to the V8.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy an AMG GT?

profiles.verdict: This is the AMG GT's core audience — buyers who want a genuine front-mid-engined V8 sports car that can take on a Porsche 911 on the road and track. Buy a C190 GT S (2017–2020) — the sweet spot of performance (£55,000–£85,000), reliability (post-2018 M178), and value (depreciation already hit). The V8 is the reason to buy this car; the four-cylinder C192 is a different proposition entirely.

The AMG GT is a future classic — especially the GT R and Black Series. The M178 V8 is one of the great AMG engines, and the C190 generation is the last pure V8 GT before hybridisation. Buy a low-mileage GT R with full history and keep it for a decade — it will appreciate. The C192's V8 models are also future classics, but they're more complex and less analog.

The GT R and GT R Pro are built for the track — active aero, rear-wheel steering, and 585 bhp from the M178. Replacement parts are expensive (£6,000–£10,000 recon engine), but the car's value (£100,000–£180,000) absorbs the cost. Budget for regular track-day maintenance — brakes, tyres, and oil changes — and you'll have one of the best track cars money can buy.

The AMG GT is not a daily driver — it's a sports car first, daily second. The C192's AWD V8 models are more daily-friendly, but the boot is small, the ride is firm, and the running costs are high. If you're buying a GT to drive every day, buy a GT 4-Door (X290) with the M256 six-cylinder instead — it's more practical and cheaper to run.

The AMG GT is a lot of car for a first-time AMG buyer — the M178 V8 has a distinct character, the car is rear-wheel drive (except C192 AWD models), and the running costs are high. The honest advice: buy an AMG C63 first (£30,000–£50,000, same M178 engine in a more practical package), get used to AMG V8 ownership, then step up to the GT. The GT is a car you grow into, not a car you start with.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My AMG GT Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: The AMG GT's economics are defined by the car's value — engine replacement cost is almost always well within the car's worth. If your C190 GT has a turbocharger actuator failure (the most common AMG GT problem in our data), a £800–£2,000 repair is a no-brainer — the car is worth £55,000–£85,000 [MB-QUOTE]. If the engine has suffered terminal bottom-end damage (rare on the M178 but possible on neglected examples), the £6,000–£10,000 recon cost is still a manageable fraction of the car's value. Use our calculator to get a personalised verdict.

trustBlock.h2: Why AMG GT Owners Trust mercedesengine.uk

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost figure on this page is drawn from real UK AMG GT enquiries and specialist interviews across 2025 — not manufacturer marketing, not forum guesswork.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: When an AMG GT's replacement cost exceeds 70% of the car's value, we say so. The honest truth? On the V8 GT, replacement almost never exceeds the threshold — but we'd still tell you if it did. That's the difference between a knowledge base and a sales page.

signals.title: Vetted Mercedes
signals.text: Benz Specialists — Our network of 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists includes AMG-specific workshops that work on M178 turbocharger actuators and timing chain tensioners every week — they're vetted specifically for AMG V8 work, not generalists who happen to take on AMG jobs.

signals.title: Part of Engine Finders
signals.text: mercedesengine.uk is part of the Engine Finders family, the UK's leading engine replacement marketplace — giving you the research depth of an independent authority with a vetted route to action when you need one.

items.question: AMG GT vs Porsche 911 — which is more reliable?
items.answer: The Porsche 911 has a reputation for bulletproof reliability — the 3.0-litre and 3.8-litre flat-six engines are well-documented and robust at high mileage. The AMG GT's M178 V8 is also reliable when maintained, but the turbocharger actuator failure (£800–£2,000) and timing chain tensioner wear (early examples) are real caveats. The honest verdict: the 911 is the more reliable, more dailyable sports car; the AMG GT is the more dramatic, more emotionally engaging car. Buy the GT for the experience; buy the 911 if you want a sports car you can use every day. Read the full comparison: AMG GT vs Porsche 911 →
